slides.title: What DML is
body: DML = Data Manipulation Language. The verbs Apex uses to change records in the database: insert, update, upsert, delete, undelete.
Real example: every Apex trigger, every async batch, every API endpoint that persists data ultimately performs DML. SOQL reads — DML writes.

vocabulary.def: Data Manipulation Language — Apex's verbs for changing records (insert/update/upsert/delete/undelete).

slides.title: insert — creating a record
body: Construct an SObject in Apex (like 'new Account()'), set its fields, then 'insert acct;'. The platform assigns the Id.
Real example: every Salesforce Web-to-Lead form that uses Apex routing ends with an insert.

slides.title: Inserting a single record
slides.intro: Type this. Construct, set, insert, read back the auto-assigned Id.
lineExplanations: Construct an empty Account in memory. Has no Id yet.
Set the required Name field.
DML: insert. Platform writes to the database, assigns an Id, and stamps it back onto our 'acct' variable.
Print the new Id — proof that the record exists in the database now.

slides.title: update — modifying existing
body: Update needs records that ALREADY have an Id. Change some fields, then 'update record;'.
Real example: the 'Edit' button on every Salesforce record page ultimately runs an update.

slides.title: Updating a record
lineExplanations: Query one Account. Now 'a' has an Id and a Name.
Mutate the Name in memory. The database hasn't changed yet — only the in-memory copy.
DML: update. The platform writes the new Name to the database row matching a.Id.

slides.title: Bulk DML — work on Lists
body: Always prefer DML on a List rather than calling it once per record. ONE 'insert leads' on a List<Lead> of 200 = ONE DML statement against the governor limit.
Real example: an integration that imports 5,000 leads. Looping 'insert lead;' 5,000 times = 5,000 DML statements = governor-limit failure. One bulk insert = 1 DML statement.

vocabulary.def: DML applied to a List of records in a single call — the bulkified pattern that respects governor limits.

slides.title: Bulk insert
slides.intro: Type this. Build a List, insert it once.
lineExplanations: Empty list ready to fill.
Construct an Account inline with Name set, append to the list. 'Name=...' is constructor-shorthand syntax for SObjects.
Two more.
And a third.
ONE DML statement creates all three records in one round-trip.

slides.title: upsert — insert or update
body: Upsert is one statement that does the right thing — if the record has an Id (or matches a key), update it; otherwise, insert a new one.
Real example: nightly imports from a partner system. You don't always know which records are new vs. existing — upsert handles both transparently.

slides.title: Upsert by external Id
slides.intro: Type this. Upsert keyed on a custom external id field.
lineExplanations: Construct a Lead with three fields.
LastName.
Company.
External_Id__c is a custom field marked as External Id.
Close construction.
upsert l External_Id__c — match on the external id; insert if no match, update if there is one.

slides.title: delete and undelete
body: delete soft-deletes (record goes to the Recycle Bin, can be restored). undelete pulls it back out.
Real example: a 'Mark as Spam' button might delete the record. An 'Oops, restore it' admin action might undelete.

slides.title: Deleting a record
slides.code:
Account a = [SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Name = 'Acme Corp' LIMIT 1];
delete a;
lineExplanations: Query one matching Account.
DML: delete. The record moves to the Recycle Bin and disappears from active queries.

slides.title: Database.insert with allOrNone
body: Default 'insert list' fails the WHOLE batch if any record is invalid. Database.insert(records, false) inserts what's valid and reports failures for the rest.
Real example: an import of 1,000 records where 3 have bad data. With allOrNone=false, you save 997 and log the 3 failures. With the default, you save zero.

vocabulary.def: DML option — false means partial success allowed, true (default) means the whole batch fails on any single error.

slides.title: Partial-success insert
slides.intro: Type this. Database.insert with allOrNone=false returns a SaveResult per record.
lineExplanations: Database.insert returns a List<SaveResult>. Each result corresponds to one record. false = allow partial success.
Loop the results.
If this record FAILED, log the error.
getErrors() returns the per-record error info. Save successes were saved; you only inspect failures.
Close.
Close.

slides.front: You're inserting 5,000 leads in a single Apex transaction. What's the safe pattern?
slides.back: Build a List<Lead>, then ONE 'insert listOfLeads;' (or Database.insert).
slides.detail: Looping 'insert lead;' once per record = 5,000 DML statements = blown governor limit. Bulk DML = 1 statement.

slides.question: You're loading partner data nightly. Some rows match existing records by an External Id; some are new. Which DML?
options: upsert — handles both insert and update in one call.
slides.explanation: upsert. It keys on a field you choose (External Id) and does the right thing per record.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Insert / update / upsert / delete / undelete, bulk DML on Lists, Database.insert with allOrNone for partial success.
Next: Test Classes — required for every Apex deployment to production, with a 75% coverage minimum.
